title: Minimum Reverse Operations
performanceTarget: top 1% execution time and overall performance
note: C++ proposals. The speed extreme coincides with the recommended parity-compressed successor-DSU BFS, so no duplicate solution-runtime.cpp is shipped. solution-memory.cpp is a genuine lower-successor-memory bitset variant.

variants.languageReason: The graph has up to 1e5 indices and the hot path is BFS plus successor deletions by parity. C++ gives native loops, compact contiguous vectors, direct LeetCode support, and no managed-runtime or interpreter overhead.

languageAlternatives.assessment: Selected. It gives the best blend of raw speed, compact vectors, path-compressed DSU, and readable LeetCode integration.
Raw arrays are competitive, but the C return signature and manual allocation add risk without improving the balanced proposal's asymptotic memory.
Native and safe, but the mutable DSU and queue plumbing are more verbose and less likely to beat C++ constants on LeetCode.
Compiled, but bounds checks and runtime overhead are less attractive for dense BFS loops over 1e5 positions.
Primitive arrays can be fast after JIT warmup, but C++ avoids managed-runtime startup, GC interaction, and larger runtime baseline.
Too much per-iteration overhead for top-percentile performance at the maximum constraints.

variants.approachJustification: Run BFS from p. For each index, derive the parity-fixed target interval [low, high]. A successor DSU over compressed parity positions enumerates only still-unvisited allowed indices, then deletes each discovered index immediately.

variants.languageReason: The recommended parity-compressed successor-DSU BFS is already the speed extreme: no explicit edge generation, no ordered tree, no hash table, and no repeated scan over visited indices.

languageAlternatives.assessment: Selected. The tight loop is native array indexing, path compression, and a manual vector queue.
Could mirror the arrays, but platform plumbing does not provide a practical speed win over optimized C++.
Native speed is possible, but checked indexing and borrow management make the hot path harder to keep minimal.
Slice bounds checks and runtime costs work against the raw-runtime objective.
JIT runtimes can be competitive, but C++ keeps startup and memory layout more predictable for percentile scoring.
Interpreter or VM overhead dominates the linear-ish algorithm at n = 1e5.

variants.approachJustification: Same file as the recommended proposal; a separate solution-runtime.cpp would be coincident and is intentionally omitted.

variants.languageReason: The memory variant needs compact bit operations, count-trailing-zero extraction, and a word-level DSU while preserving the LeetCode C++ vector return type. C++ provides this with minimal container overhead.

languageAlternatives.assessment: Selected. 64-bit bitsets, __builtin_ctzll, compact vectors, and direct platform support make the memory-focused implementation practical.
Can use the same bit operations, but manual return ownership and allocation plumbing add risk for little measured-memory benefit.
Strong memory control, but mutable bitsets plus word-level DSU are more verbose and do not reduce the mandatory answer or queue.
Bit operations are available, but slices plus runtime and GC baseline are larger.
Primitive arrays avoid boxing, but managed-runtime baseline memory is higher than C++.
Object-heavy numeric storage is unsuitable for a memory-extreme solution.

variants.approachJustification: Run the same BFS, but store unvisited positions as parity-compressed bitsets and skip empty words with a word-level successor DSU. This shrinks the removable-state representation while preserving correctness.
variants.spaceComplexity: O(n), with O(n / 64) DSU words plus O(n) mandatory answer and queue

reasoningSummary: Reversing a length-k subarray containing index i maps the 1 to j = 2L + k - 1 - i. Valid starts L form an interval, so valid targets form [max(i-k+1,k-1-i), min(i+k-1,2n-k-1-i)] with step 2. BFS gives minimum operation counts; parity-separated successor structures enumerate each unvisited allowed target once without O(k) scans or O(log n) tree operations.
timeComplexity: O(n alpha(n)) recommended/runtime, O(n + (n / 64) alpha(n / 64)) memory
